chunks.text: Hang on.

And welcome to the other end of the table. From the frying pan into the fire. So, we'll leave it in your hands. We've got you for an hour. You will have around 10 minutes for opening remarks, and then we'll get into some questions. Thank you very much, Chair, and it's great to be here.

It's a little bit different being at this end of the table, and I was sort of reminiscing. It's like, "Hmm," it's like the frying pan into the fire maybe as well. But great to be in front of the best select committee in Parliament as well. You got it. So thanks very much. Good start. Just introduce who's here beside me. We've got Jen Wright, acting CE, and on my right here we've got Nick Dixon, Enterprise Services as well. So, without further ado, I will just launch into some brief introductory remarks.

I'd just like to begin by saying that as a relatively new minister in this portfolio, I've really enjoyed coming on board and getting to know the portfolio and the breadth of the work that Land Information New Zealand delivers. I would also just like to acknowledge the support of my predecessor, the Honourable Chris Penk, and the support of Jen and the wider team behind me as I get to grips with the portfolio.

It's been a valuable opportunity to better understand how LINZ supports New Zealanders from managing overseas investment in a way that benefits New Zealand, to enabling the productive use of Crown land, protecting our natural environment and tackling complex biosecurity challenges. As you're all aware, LINZ is responsible for around 1.5 million hectares of Crown land, including properties in the Treaty Settlements Land Bank, pastoral leases, and lakes and riverbeds. It also underpins New Zealand's property rights system, typically recording more than

600,000 instruments against titles, which is about 1,650 for every day of the year, and approving over 11,000 survey plans each and every year. In addition, LINZ is progressing work to 3D map more than 40% of New Zealand's coastline. I've also been encouraged by the progress LINZ is making in modernising how it works, including the use of digital tools and AI to improve services and productivity across the organisation.

Today, I'll touch on a number of key areas, including the overseas investment, Crown pastoral land reforms, and freshwater weed control, some of which may answer in advance questions you may have. Earlier this year, the government made improvements to the Overseas Investment Act. These changes balance this government's view that most overseas investment is low risk and should be encouraged while still protecting New Zealand's national interest and our most sensitive assets like farmland and houses.

As the regulatory agency for the overseas investment regime, LINZ has implemented changes to its systems and processes to deliver the government's expectation that applications are processed more quickly under the new test that gives investors certainty faster. And as a result, the latest figures show that over 90% of applications are assessed within 50% of the timeframe set for them under regulation.

LINZ is also maintaining public transparency around foreign investment into New Zealand with summaries of all overseas investment decisions published on the LINZ website the month following each decision. And in addition, LINZ is also ensuring the rules are enforced. I was very pleased to see a strong response to the discovery of an Australian investor trying to circumvent the rules and illegally buy sensitive farmland.

This resulted in $440,000 in penalties for the foreign investor and the New Zealander who was their associate and frontman. The Public Works Amendment Bill was introduced in December last year and is now ready for its second reading. Changes to this act will provide fairer compensation, including incentive payments for all acquisitions and encouraging early agreement to make it easier for landowners and agencies to navigate the system, helping accelerate the delivery of infrastructure such as roads, schools and hospitals.

And the bill also provides a new process for acquiring land more quickly that will also support communities to recover more quickly from emergencies. However, those provisions will only be activated when necessary and will not be needed for all state of emergency events. A key priority has been progressing the Crown Land Legislation Amendment Bill, which has now been introduced to Parliament.

The reforms are about unlocking opportunity while maintaining appropriate stewardship of the land. Under the bill, Crown pastoral leaseholders will have greater ability to maximize land use and pursue new economic opportunities alongside pastoral farming. And this includes activities like farm shops selling products grown or reared on farm, small scale hospitality ventures, and potential renewable energy projects. These changes will support economic resilience across regional New Zealand while recognizing and maintaining the unique

values of Crown pastoral land. LINZ is also making strong progress in its digital transformation, including the use of automation and AI to improve how it delivers services. AI is already helping staff work more efficiently, from supporting faster responses in the customer hub to analyzing large volumes of information and improving the quality and consistency of written advice. These improvements are delivering practical results, including reduced call handling times and more timely service for customers.

And this focus on smarter, more efficient ways of working is also reflected in continued investment in systems like Landonline, which is increasing automation across the property system and making transactions more efficient, accurate, and reliable for New Zealanders. Another critical role LINZ plays is in supporting New Zealand's maritime safety and trade by providing trusted hydrographic information, charts, and navigation data from across New Zealand, the South West Pacific, and the Ross Sea of Antarctica.

Last year, LINZ and Maritime New Zealand received $28.6 million of government funding for the Maritime Digital Transformation program. This program will transform and transition maritime navigation from paper charts to new digital data-driven products aligned with the global standard, ensuring safe and efficient maritime travel into the future.

Additionally, the Southern Positioning Augmentation Network, SouthPAN, continues to deliver benefits with its early open services with users across maritime, construction, infrastructure, primary production, and local government. As uptake grows over time, SouthPAN is expected to deliver $864 million in economic benefits over 20 years through improved productivity, reduced risk, and greater confidence in location information.

Uh, freshwater weed control is another area where LINZ is delivering important results. I had the opportunity to visit Lake Wanaka earlier with the chair, uh, this month, and it was encouraging to f- see firsthand the progress being made. And it's believed that the work in this lake is the largest, bear with me if I get this wrong, Lagarosiphon, uh, eradication program in the world.

LINZ has also recently undertaken preventative work in the Kaweroa River, laying the equivalent of eight rugby fields of hessian matting to stop the spread of the weed into Lake Waikaremoana. And this work is vital for local communities, business, tourism, and the environment. Overall, LINZ is delivering practical outcomes that balance economic opportunity with good environmental stewardship, and I look forward to continuing this work. And with that, I'm open for questions.

Right. We'll go to Jo with the first question. Thank you, Minister. It's nice to, uh, see you, uh, today and, um, all the best for your hearing in upcoming hearings. Okay, so Budget 2026- Mm-hmm... has cuts of 8.4 million over the forecast period for LINZ, which I see budget documents claim will be from efficiencies across the department's Crown land, location-based information, overseas investment, and property rights appropriations. Can you tell the committee what these specific efficiencies are? Because in your answer to question 2022, uh, sorry,

22- Uh, 20-... of the pre-hearing questions, you note that the primary risks arising from cost reductions relate to reduced operational flexibility and capability in enabling functions. Can you tell us more about those reductions? Uh, bear with me, I'll just find- Yep... the one for 22. Um, yeah, so look, in terms of Budget 2026 savings, uh, LINZ submitted 2.1 million, uh, in Budget 2026 baseline reductions. Um, and then LINZ will achieve this through finding efficiencies and prioritization of critical work.

And LINZ will continue to evolve its operating model toward leaner structures and cross-functional teams. And I might just pass to- Yeah, sure... Jen. So, um, while those efficiencies came out of those appropriations, um, those were appropriations that back office activities were covered by. So what we've done is rationalized our, our management. Um, one of the Dep Sec roles was, um, revised, and, um, Nick is in a role that's, um, also got a functional responsibility, so we took out a Dep Sec role. We took out a number of, um,

management layers and me- and, and, um, brought together teams to... And then we also looked at particularly our, um, technology contract, and made reductions there. So the 2.1 we were able to realize without having any impact on frontline services.

So while it looks like it c- it has to come out of those appropriations, that's where the back office is funded from. Okay. So- Does that make sense? Yeah. Yep. Have they all been identified and confirmed as realistic, and you know that you'll be able to get the savings if, that you expect from these efficiencies?

If not, should you be concerned th- should be- we be concerned that you're saying you'll be able to save 8.4 million and yet you don't know from exactly where? Uh, no, look, I have every confidence, um, that LINZ will continue to maintain our frontline services. And, you know, I've been given assurance that that is the case, so. So the question was, uh, do you know that you'll be getting the savings you expect from these efficiencies?

Do you want me to answer that? Yeah. Yeah. So we have removed those roles now, so that from the 1st of July, when these savings take effect, um, the, the functions have reduced, and the rationalizations occurred. One last up. Yep. Thank you. Um- No sweat on that... do any of these efficiencies and cost saving measures involve greater user pays methods at all? If so- Of what methods?

What are they? User pays. Oh, user pays. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Yeah. Um, so obviously there's a, there's a large component of LINZ's cost recovery. Yeah. Um, and bear with me just a sec here. Um, the 156.2 million, .29 million departmental appropriation under vote LINZ is largely funded through third party revenue, and that includes regulated fees and charges under the, uh, Land Information New Zealand Fees and Charges Regulations, along with other funding arrangements.

So will they be increasing? No. Perfect. Thank you. You, Susanna. Um, supplementary. So just to talk about workforce and, um, supplementing what Steve already asked, really keen to understand in terms of the reduction of head count, um, and the way that teams or roles have been repurposed.

Um, has there been any impact on some of these projects that are of significance across LINZ, um, including the Land Online transaction system, the South Pan, and also the Marine Digital Transformation. Um, do any of the roles that have been impacted, um, have any, uh, part to play within these projects?

No. They all have, um, separate funding lines, and they're all, um, resourced and funded from the project money. Um, just to supplement. And thinking about, 'cause, uh, LINZ is quite a, um, specialist- Yep... area of work, and thinking about our workforce- Mm-hmm... um, what is the plan to both look to, uh, keep an open door for the talent that might be needed while still trying to make the cost efficiencies and the cuts that are required?

Yeah. As you identified, um, we have quite a specialist workforce, and those specialist roles at this stage aren't impacted. So we've been able to retain their capability and attract it as well. Kia ora. Jo, you had another question. Yeah. Thank you. Um, so your answer to pre-hearing question number 26, uh, significant risks, notes that LINZ is operating in a constrained and increasingly complex environment.

Key pressures include ongoing fiscal restraint, rising costs of property and professional services, and heightened expectations from central and local government, communities, and iwi partners. Can you walk us through what these... what are these key pressures? Yeah, I, I could probably give you a little bit of an overview, and then I might pass to Jen. Um, yes, look, there's no doubt, um, there's been some inflationary pressures.

Um, and so, uh, in terms of the properties, there's a broad range of properties that LINZ, uh, manages and oversees. Um, and, you know, I've been, uh, very interested in, um, in understanding how we manage those and how we potentially divest, um, ourselves of those properties that we actually don't need as well. So obviously, if you're not managing them, you can reduce the management, uh, pressures and costs.

Mm-hmm. So, um, we're looking at how we can, um, both rationalize the service and, um, make it more efficient. Um, I can get Nic to talk about how we're utilizing and planning to utilize AI as well. So shall I hand over? Sure. Um, LINZ has been on quite a journey over the last year. Uh, I think the environment has shifted from, you know, the risk of using AI to, uh, the risk of not using AI.

So that's, uh, taught LINZ to navigate its new environment, make sure that we've got the right, um, guardrails and security and safety, while also looking for those opportunities to enhance our customer service, um, while being more efficient and productive. So we have an internal AI accelerator that has been stood up, and the purpose of that accelerator is to scale high-value, uh, service delivery, where there are situations that there's, uh, lots of manual, repetitive tasks, or situations that provide quite a lot of

administrative burden for our people. So either speeding up delivery and service for our customer while also being more efficient. Mm-hmm. So there's multiple opportunities that the accelerator's looking at. Uh, the first is, uh, customer-facing services, where again, there's high volumes of manual, repetitive work that can be automated.

The second is in areas like, uh, ministerial services, like procurement, that again, it's repetitive tasks that can easily be automated. And the final is in deep analysis, uh, for example, in our policy submissions. The work that we're doing in terms of analyzing large volumes of submissions, um, looking at our summarization and advice-giving using deeper analysis, using AI, means we can work faster.

So that's where some of those, um, service levels can stay the same while using efficiency. In the policy space, we're actually taking a leading role. We're now supporting other agencies, uh, with this, uh, policy submission work, so we think it's gonna have broad benefits. Yeah, Sue. Yeah, I do. So you talked about, in that, in that, uh, number 26 answer, the heightened expectations in, uh, from central and local government communities and iwi partners.

Um, what are those heightened expectations? Can you talk us through that? You know, I, I think, um, you know, it's, it's very complex in terms of, um, you know, particularly relates to the Land Bank. It's very complex and, um, you know, there's a number of properties that, um, LINZ supervisors or managers, um, sometimes it's very hard to ascertain how long they need to manage a property for.

It could be a month, a year, or five or 10 years. It's, it is challenging and it is complex. Um, but if I could just duck back to the AI, a, a very good example of the use of AI is, and I mentioned it in my opening remarks, is reducing the, uh, call handling times in the customer hub from 16 minutes to 12 minutes. Which may not sound much, but it's, uh, four minutes, and which is actually a 25% increase in efficiency, which is a very good use of AI.

I know. Yeah. I just, I'm sorry, I just don't quite understand your explanation about... 'Cause the question was, what are the heightened expectations from central and local government communities and iwi partners that you noted in your answer. Yep. Mm-hmm. So what are those? I'll get, um, Sonya to come and talk about, uh, her property space. Thank you.

Thanks, Nick. Uh, kia ora koutou. Ko Sonya Wikitera ahau. Uh, my substantive role is Head of Crown Property. Um, we've got a number of, um, engagements, particularly in the community and with iwi stakeholders and local stakeholders, uh, where we are discussing, of course, um, the minister talked about aquatic weed- Mm.

Um, terrestrial weed work, wilding pine work. Um, and I think that probably reflects the heightened desire for people in those communities- Thank you... actually to be rid of that kind of- Thank you... um, pest and weed species. Yeah. And the great work that actually we end up doing- Mm... not just with LINZ, um, but with m-many of the other government agencies and also our partners.

Thank you. Yeah You had a primary, Huhana I did. You might as well stay there, aren't you? So, I was wanting to talk about Crown lands administered by LINZ, and in particular, thinking about the land bank. Seeing that the budget's been halved, basically, from 30 mil down to 16 mil-ish, in terms of the acquiring of whenua and the preparation of lands for settlement.

What's that going to look like in terms of the work program available for the acquiring of new lands for land bank? Yeah. I think the information that you've got, and I'm wondering if the CFO might jump up, is from iPET adjustments that are made from year to year.

Mm-hmm. We have quite a stable appropriation that we use for purchasing of property to go into the land bank. Yeah. And it's been fairly stable for years. If we don't, well not just us, but Te Tari Whakatoe, if they don't spend the total appropriation- Mm... then they look for an iPET to transfer that to subsequent years so that they can keep that pool of funding available for the NPM purchases.

Mm. Or recently, more private purchases. So, um- And how much is that if we've had budget stability across that whenua? I feel like I'm going to look backwards. About 14 million, so. Oh, 14 mil across them all two? Correct. 40. 14. 14. 14 mil, cool. Okay then, so if we say that we have therefore budget stability for acquisition of land and the management of land within the land bank, wanting to understand, in terms of maybe, I don't know who's appropriate to guide me through the process, but when we have lands that are included in a settlement,

and then it's been prepared, it's been confirmed, signed, all of that. We've had legislation go through the house. What is the pathway for final change in title? How long does that take? I think that's Simon. Yeah. Look- So I've heard it's years, but you know, this is just me just speculating.

It can be years in the making. Mm. So, once the treaty legislation is passed, so obviously there's a huge process that goes on beforehand. There is the negotiation process- Mm... which Te Tari Whakatoe manage, which I'm sure you're aware of. But also iwi will go through Crown owned land, not just what's in the land bank.

Mm. But also Crown owned land from MOE, and other government agencies to have a look at. Mm. And to make choices about whether or not they want those to be included as part of their treaty settlement. Mm. So we don't have all of the land that might be available for a treaty settlement, we just hold the land bank- Mm... which is the excess, no longer used Crown assets, I suppose.

Mm. And so how long does it take for your specific types of land to be transferred over to the iwi- Yeah... the title? So most treaty settlements will have settlement day, or they might have early redress properties, and they may go across before the legislation's signed. The final legislation. And then I think once the legislation's signed, there's also another grouping of assets, which will be settlement day properties, which will go a- across to iwi within about 20 working days- Mm... I think it is.

And then most treaty settlements will have time frames in them, for deferred settlement properties, which usually are around two to five years. Mm. And then most treaty settlements will have a right of first refusal, which could then be on that land title forever. Yeah. Yeah.

Yeah, so dep- it depends on which grouping. Yeah. We're obviously trying to ensure that we meet the deadlines that are set out in the treaty settlement. So we make sure that we work heavily on the early release and the settlement day properties. And then we work with each of the PSGEs to work through the list of the DSP properties or the RFR properties.

Recently heard anecdotally from an iwi through settlement- Do you want me to-... that had started getting charged rates on properties that had yet had their title transferred over to them. Is that a council issue or a LINZ issue? It's quite interesting that they started getting charged rates from the time of settlement, yet they hadn't received the title over.

Mm. So I- I could name the iwi. Oh, no, you can probably provide that to me maybe later. Yes. You're allowed to do that. Yes. That would typically not occur. Yeah. Mm-hmm. So as we- Is it a council issue? As we know... Mm. So there could be a couple of things at play here. So of course, when the title is transferred, that's normally when the council's getting the information about, you know, who to charge for the rates.

So that's usually when that occurs. Mm. Sometimes our tenants are responsible for rates as part of their lease agreement. So there could be a bit of, yeah, a bit of information in that, which would be helpful. So it's quite specific. It's a specific question you would write to the- Yeah... write to the... Yeah, if you could. Mm. Dana had her sub on the- Well, mine was kind of related to Huhana's question actually, but was about the number, the sheer weighty number of treaty settlement obligations that LINZ has.

Mm. And I know that there are literally hundreds, if not thousands. I think thousands is actually the number. But just what focus goes on to those as an organization in terms of getting them through the process so that they are keeping things moving. Because it is, I think, the greatest number of all of the treaty obligations That you have to deal with.

So we do have responsibility for inputting into Te Haera, which is the system that TPK manages, obviously, to ensure that all of the commitments are being done. LINZ has an interesting role because not only do we have properties that we need to transfer through settlement day, deferred settlement, or RFR process, but LINZ is also involved at the very last stage with all of those other Crown agencies in the title transfer.

Mm. So I'm going to- So that LINZ has an inflated number- Mm... um, of obligations in Te Haeta because we are either involved in the process or we are at the very end of the process for every government agency who are doing the, the sale, um, through to, um, our PSGs. Okay.

So I know that, uh, we have about 77% of our commitments have been completed in Te Haeta. Uh, I don't believe we have any that are overdue. Obviously, we still have a number that we're working through. Great. Yeah. Thank you. Stuart. Yeah. You have a question. Thank you, and welcome. I've, I've got a question about, uh, LINZ blocks, land lock blocks, uh, particularly in North Canterbury.

I know you've seen some. Uh, and also the sale process of those blocks. I, I... Tidying them up 'cause they're not actually being utilized by LINZ, they're just, uh, actually scrub land where amongst good farmland. And, but when the process begins, and I've got another incident, uh, instance where, um, the land was, uh, the deal was sort of going through, and then- Of course... LINZ decided to, uh, that they needed to do a competitive tender at the last minute.

And so sort of withdrew and started again. So, uh, is that sort of thing, I wouldn't have thought it was usual, and it's not usual b- business practice. Uh, is that, is that under control or...? Yeah, look, it's not something I can specifically comment on that, that- Yeah... particular situation, but, um, yeah, yeah, look, I'm certainly interested, as I said in my opening remarks, about understanding, uh, what surplus land there is, and there's no point to my mind actually, um, managing that if there's no interest in, in, if it's not going into the land bank.

Mm. And it's surplus to requirements, and I, uh, you know, because there's management costs involved. But in terms of, as that specific example- There's no management. It's not being managed. Yeah. Yeah. So look, unless there's anyone else wants to make a comment on that then- No, if you want to give us that detail we can- I, I've given- Yeah, yeah... given the minister the details.

Okay. Okay. But, um, slightly different but related in terms of the overseas investment- Mm-hmm... um, Act. So there's one that's just been publicized and, uh, recently a sale in Marlborough, the Kenepuru Sound, for a feedlot. Mm. Now, I'm highly skeptical about the feedlot, given the likely need to be a wharf build or built, or the roads won't be capable of bringing the feed and all that sort of stuff, all those questions.

Mm. And there are other... Uh, so I don't, don't need you to comment on that in particular. The question is, what happens in the process when an approval is given, say for a feedlot in a- Yes... a highly remote place that seems uneconomic to anyone else or, uh, not sensible. That, uh, that, that is followed through as a condition.

And, uh, what is the timeframe for, uh, that to be delivered? Is that, is that a common thing or is it a by agreement? And what is the, uh, what, what are the consequences of not delivering on that commitment? Yeah, very good questions. Actually, it's something I've been particularly interested in, is, um, uh, uh, to your question, um, is, you know, when those conditions are posed, how are they monitored and for how long?

Mm. And exactly to your point, what are the consequences? Mm. And I think there's a secondary part to that, is what happens when people try to circumvent- Yes... that, that, that process as well. I think there's sort of two quite separate things. And I would make the observation that it is a privilege, uh, being able to invest in sensitive land in New Zealand, and that comes with conditions, and those conditions should be adhered to. So I think, John.

Petro. Yeah, Petro. Yes. So the, e- e- every consent is subject to conditions of some kind. They vary depending on the consent pathway and the subject matter. Um, I can't speak to the specific case that you've raised, but a case like that would generally have conditions that require the underlying development or business to actually be developed and implemented.

Uh, and the failure to deliver that is tied again to a very specific condition that requires those assets to be disposed of at, at LINZ's discretion if, if that business isn't developed. Um, depending on the circumstances around that, we've got a, a wider ability to take action. We can take civil and criminal proceedings against people. We can secure penalties. Um, looking back 10 or 11 years, um, I think we've taken over $13 million in penalties and costs from investors who've either breached conditions or who've, um, as the minister, um, says, have, uh, have

attempted to circumvent the rules in some ways. Um, the, a, a big part of the back end of that is in the publicity of that. It's not just a, a quiet sort of slap on the wrist in a, in a back corner. Um, the publicity and the, and the public attention of this is important. We want other investors to know that if they behave well, if they're good investors, if they're adding value to New Zealand, um, that they should be encouraged to invest, but that there are rules that exist to protect New Zealand, um, particularly New Zealand's national interest, and they will be enforced.

So just, uh, one final supplement if I can. So, um, a- and using this example that I raised. Mm. Um, there is no consent, nor has there been one applied for, for that activity. Would that be usual? Would it, what would normally follow if I was applying for a consent, um, to, uh, to purchase sensitive land as a foreigner, and that, um, that sensitive land was to do an activity that would require a consent of some kind? Which, um, would that need to be in place, or is, what happens if I, I, 'cause there isn't one, uh, they don't get the consent, then what

happens in the process? So it, it, it depends on the risk posed by a transaction, both the risk of- A perceived risk of non-compliance, so something that's a bit out there, we would tend to condition a little more prescriptively. On the other end of the scale, a known investor who's got a long track record of compliance, we would tend to condition a little less prescriptively or a little more light-handedly. They're just a reflection of the risk posed by the transaction. At the higher risk end of the scale, which I think

is the end that you're probably interested in, we would look to potentially condition a number of milestones along that development cycle. So firstly, potentially the requirement to apply for resource building consents, other consents may be required. A requirement to obtain it, or potentially the requirement to break ground on a development, that would be common, say, for new housing development.

A requirement to complete that development, and then again, in the case of housing, to then on-sell it at the end. That gives us a number of milestones along the way where we can intervene if we need to. It's relatively unusual that we would need to. At that lower risk end, we probably wouldn't have as many of those milestones. It's adding cost for us and it's adding cost for the investor, and there's no real risk-prevent need to intervene at that level.

I'm not on a witch hunt, but I was just- No, no, no... just wondering, and is that publicised? It's a fair question. To what level is that publicised, or is it not publicised, those conditions? The conditions are subject to the Official Information Act. They're generally not proactively released other than in- Okay... high-profile cases. But people ask for them all the time, and when they do, they're generally released, and when they're released, we then proactively publish them on our website.

Thank you very much for your fulsome answer. Sue's had a sup. Oh, I've got a quick supplementary. So you talked about the penalties and also the ability of LINZ to take the land back. Mm-hmm. So, and you talked about penalties being up to $13 million. So is that when you miss a milestone, does that automatically trigger a penalty?

No, it doesn't automatically trigger a penalty. It triggers our ability to seek a penalty. Those penalties are all sought through the High Court. We take proceedings against, or High Court or District Court, we take proceedings against people, and the court orders those penalties- Well-... to be paid... that process can be quite an expensive exercise for the taxpayer. Well, it's not as expensive as you'd think.

We're very good at what we do. It's unusual for somebody to challenge us when we take those proceedings. We've got a team of really good investigators who put these cases together. And virtually all of the... The penalties aren't necessarily agreed, but it's unusual for us to take a matter to court and for people not to essentially accept that they've broken the rules. We've got a pretty good track record of going against those people. And on the cost to the taxpayer, these are round figures, but the penalties that we've recovered have met about three-quarters of the

Crown's cost overall of that entire enforcement framework. So if you look at that Crown-funded element of LINZ's compliance and enforcement framework, about three-quarters of that has been recovered to date from the penalties that we've received. That's trending up. We've currently got five cases either before the court or on the verge of being introduced, and three cases currently before the court where we're waiting for penalties, and that will see that percentage of the total spend trend up towards 100% or more over time, I

think. Okay. So say, for example, I bought some land. I was overseas, got permission to buy some land, said I was going to plant a kiwifruit orchard, never got a consent, never planted the kiwifruit orchard. At what point and what's the timeframe for when you'd kick in and say, "Sue, you need to give it back to us," or, "Here's your fines, and then at what point do we give it back?"

So it- Like to give it back... it depends on the circumstances. So assume you got consent, so we've said you can buy it, and then you haven't planted it. Our first question would be why didn't you plant it? And if you didn't plant it because cyclone Gabrielle- Well, I haven't even got a consent. Sorry? I haven't even got a consent. Oh, if you haven't got any consent, as soon as we found out about that, we're going to want to know why you didn't get consent.

And we would look to move reasonably quickly after that, if we could. Okay. Thank you. It's good to know that the- Yeah. Jamie. Yeah, thanks, Chair. Thanks, Minister. It's pretty much a spinoff of what we're discussing now, actually. I'm concerned to see that despite this government's push to increase foreign land sales, you've actually cut the appropriation for compliance and administration of the overseas investment regime. Can you tell the committee the reasoning behind that cut? Because it's the only part of the appropriation that you've

actually cut. Why not make these savings out of ministerial servicing, which has remained the same, rather than enforcement? Bruce, uh - I think it's Bruce. It's Bruce... sorry, we'll get our CFO to- Yeah... to speak for a question. So, you're up, Bruce. It's in the CFO fund.

So the only actual reduction that we've made in overseas investment was $100,000 as part of the cost savings initiatives. The changes that you're actually seeing in there actually reflect the fact that this is largely third-party funded.
